query: is belimumab approved for use in lupus nephritis

retrieved_docs.title: Lupus Nephritis -- Treatment / Management -- New Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Belimumab is a soluble B-cell activating factor (BAFF) antibody that was approved in 2011 for treating active SLE, and it was also approved in 2020 for induction therapy in lupus nephritis, in addition to standard MMF/cyclophosphamide/steroid protocols. Belimumab is a fully human monoclonal IgG1λ antibody that neutralizes BAFF and has demonstrated improved outcomes when used with standard protocols. The Belimumab International Study in Lupus Nephritis (BLISS-LN) study trial demonstrated its effectiveness in lupus nephritis, leading to its approval for use. [46] [63]

retrieved_docs.title: Lupus Nephritis -- Treatment / Management -- New Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: In the past 4 years, several new agents have been studied or approved for use in SLE and lupus nephritis, paving the way for a multitargeted approach that combines standard therapies with novel treatments addressing different mechanisms of action. We are likely entering an era where targeted therapies will enable improved personalization based on laboratory, demographic, and biopsy-specific factors. In addition to standard treatment, the 2 drugs approved for use in lupus nephritis include belimumab and voclosporin.

retrieved_docs.title: Lupus Nephritis -- Treatment / Management -- New Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: A theory suggests that while rituximab depletes B lymphocytes, elevated BAFF levels following treatment may trigger lupus flares. To investigate this, the BEAT LUPUS trial compared belimumab to placebo in patients with refractory disease who had previously received rituximab. The trial found significantly lower anti-dsDNA antibody levels and a reduction in lupus nephritis flares in patients treated with belimumab. [64] Additionally, belimumab may be particularly beneficial for patients with high disease activity and those with cutaneous, musculoskeletal, or serologic manifestations of lupus. [61]

retrieved_docs.title: Anifrolumab  -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Anifrolumab is indicated for treating adult patients with moderate to severe systemic lupus erythematosus receiving standard therapy. Anifrolumab is not recommended in patients with severe active lupus nephritis or severe active central nervous system lupus, as the drug's efficacy has not been evaluated in these patient populations.

retrieved_docs.title: Lupus Nephritis -- Treatment / Management -- Phase-3 trials
retrieved_docs.content: Several medications currently undergoing phase-3 trials show promising results. Anifrolumab, an IFN receptor antagonist, has demonstrated improved parameters when used alongside standard therapy protocols. The drug blocks the downstream activation of B- and T lymphocytes, dendritic cells, and epithelial cells. Anifrolumab was approved in 2021 for the treatment of moderate-to-severe SLE. [9] Atacicept is a fusion protein that combines the transmembrane activator calcium modulator and cyclophilin ligand interactor (TACI) receptor, a BAFF receptor, with the Fc region of IgG. This molecule can bind and neutralize both BAFF and APRIL, which may offer increased efficacy compared to treatments targeting BAFF alone. Telitacicept shares a similar mechanism of action. Obinutuzumab, ocrelizumab, and epratuzumab are antibodies that target surface receptors on CD20 and CD22 B cells. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Lupus Nephritis -- Treatment / Management -- Phase-3 trials
retrieved_docs.content: Ianalumab is a dual-action human monoclonal antibody that targets B lymphocytes expressing BAFF and has shown effectiveness in Sjögren syndrome. Deucravacitinib is a Janus kinase inhibitor that also modulates type-I IFN-associated gene expression and is believed to be more specific and targeted in its actions. Sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors have proven effective in reducing proteinuria and mortality across various renal diseases. A large cohort study found that this class of medications reduces the risk of developing lupus nephritis in patients with SLE. [18] [46] Povetacicept is a dual BAFF/APRIL antagonist that has recently been tested in humans and shown to be well-tolerated. This is being studied for its potential in treating antibody-related autoimmune diseases, including lupus nephritis and IgA nephropathy. Preliminary results indicate a reduction in specific antibody types. [69]

retrieved_docs.title: Lupus Nephritis -- Treatment / Management -- New Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Voclosporin is a calcineurin inhibitor that inhibits interleukin 2, decreasing T-cell activation. This drug is more potent than tacrolimus or cyclosporine and is a more stable molecule, so monitoring levels is unnecessary. [65] [66] Belimumab appears less nephrotoxic than voclosporin and can be used with impaired renal function. On the other hand, voclosporin may be more effective in patients with proteinuria greater than 3.0 g/d. [67] [68] Both voclosporin and belimumab have been associated with more rapid tapering of glucocorticoids. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Voclosporin -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Voclosporin, a novel immunosuppressive medication, is approved for treating and managing lupus nephritis (LN) within the class of calcineurin inhibitors (CNIs). LN is a common manifestation of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), an autoimmune disease affecting various organs. Approximately 40% of SLE patients may develop LN. The primary goal of LN treatment is to preserve kidney function, reduce mortality, and improve the patient's quality of life while minimizing treatment-related adverse events. A timely response to therapy, as indicated by a reduction in proteinuria, may predict long-term outcomes, including the risk of SLE flares, end-stage kidney disease, and mortality. In the United States, voclosporin received FDA approval in 2021 for treating active LN in adults, combined with background immunosuppressive therapy. This CME activity overviews the indications, actions, and contraindications for voclosporin. It also emphasizes the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and key factors such as dosing, pharmacokinetics, contraindications, and monitoring that are essential for healthcare team members involved in treating patients with lupus nephritis using voclosporin.

retrieved_docs.title: Voclosporin -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Voclosporin was approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in the USA on January 22, 2021, specifically for the treatment of adults with biopsy-proven lupus nephritis (LN) class III, IV, or V (class V alone or in combination with class III or IV). It is intended to be utilized in conjunction with background immunosuppressive therapy as part of the treatment regimen for LN. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biologic Response Modifiers (BRMs) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: TNF inhibitors, e.g., infliximab, etanercept, adalimumab, certolizumab, and golimumab has been FDA approved for use in RA, ulcerative colitis (UC), Crohn disease (CD), AS, JIA, plaque psoriasis and, psoriatic arthritis. Adalimumab also has approval for hidradenitis suppurativa and uveitis. Non-FDA-approved indications include pyoderma gangrenosum, pustular psoriasis, and GVHD(etanercept). Interleukin-1 inhibitor, e.g., anakinra, is FDA approved for RA and neonatal-onset multisystem inflammatory disease. IL-2 inhibitor, e.g., aldesleukin, is used in metastatic renal cell carcinoma and melanoma. Tocilizumab is an interleukin-6 inhibitor used in giant cell arteritis, JIA, and RA. Secukinumab that targets interleukin-17 is FDA approved for psoriasis (plaque and arthritis), and AS. Ustekinumab blocks interleukin 12/23 and has been FDA-approved for plaque psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and CD. Abatacept (T-cell co-stimulation blocker) has approval for use in RA, JIA, and Psoriatic arthritis. Rituximab is FDA approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia, non-Hodgkins lymphoma, RA, granulomatous polyangiitis, and pemphigus vulgaris. Off label uses include Burkitt lymphoma, CNS lymphoma, GVHD, lupus nephritis, and Hodgkin lymphoma. Tofacitinib is Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors indicated for psoriatic arthritis, RA, and UC. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Lupus Nephritis -- Treatment / Management -- Induction Therapy for Classes III and IV Lupus Nephritis
retrieved_docs.content: If there is no improvement or sufficient renal response after 6 months, therapy is often switched to an alternative agent, or rituximab is administered. [45] Although rituximab is frequently used in the treatment of lupus nephritis—possibly due to the perception of fewer adverse effects—the LUNAR trial did not demonstrate a statistically significant benefit when rituximab was added to standard MMF therapy in patients with active proliferative lupus nephritis, although a nonsignificant trend toward improvement was observed. [18] [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Anifrolumab  -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: No clinical trial data exist on anifrolumab use in pediatrics, pregnancy, and patients with renal/hepatic impairments. Additionally, researchers excluded patients with active severe lupus nephritis or neuropsychiatric SLE in clinical trials. Anifrolumab should not be administered in this subset of populations currently.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcineurin Inhibitors -- Indications -- Voclosporin
retrieved_docs.content: Voclosporin, a novel medication within the class of immunosuppressants, received FDA approval in 2021 for its oral formulation, which is meant to be used in conjunction with other immunosuppressants for treating active lupus nephritis in adults. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Voclosporin -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The management of autoimmune diseases, such as SLE/LN, is a complex process that imposes a significant cost burden on both patients and the healthcare system. Voclosporin, a novel immunosuppressive agent, is used in combination with background immunosuppressive therapy for patients with lupus nephritis. The utilization of background immunosuppressive therapies in conjunction with voclosporin is a critical aspect of LN management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1544
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, which are classified under the broader category of داء الكولاجين-الوعائي (collagen vascular disease), can be treated with specific medications. Prednisone is recognized as a therapeutic option for managing systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, and hydroxychloroquine is also noted for its efficacy in this condition. In contrast, the drugs trioxsalen and zlato are contraindicated in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders and should be avoided due to potential adverse effects. Awareness of these treatment options and contraindications is essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 437
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders is recognized as a subtype of vascular collagen disease (داء الكولاجين-الوعائي) and encompasses lupus nephritis (التهاب الكلية الذئبي) as a specific organ manifestation. Hydroxychlorochin (hydroxychloroquine) may be employed to treat systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, while the agents trioxsalen and zlato are contraindicated in patients with this disease group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2784
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxychlorochin may be used to treat systemic lupus erythematosus and other connective tissue disorders, offering a therapeutic option for patients with these autoimmune conditions. In contrast, both trioxsalen and zlato are contraindicated in individuals who have systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, indicating that these agents should be avoided because they can exacerbate disease activity or cause harmful effects. Careful selection of medication based on these contraindications and therapeutic potentials is essential for safe and effective management of patients with systemic lupus erythematosus and related connective tissue disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1766
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, which are classified under the broader category داء الكولاجين-الوعائي (collagen vascular disease), can be managed with specific pharmacologic agents. Methylprednisolon acetát is reported to have therapeutic potential for treating systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, and hydroxychlorochin (hydroxychloroquine) is also used for this purpose. Conversely, the drugs trioxsalen and zlato are contraindicated in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, indicating that their use may exacerbate the disease or pose safety risks. Recognizing these treatment options and contraindications is essential for optimizing patient care in this autoimmune condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2628
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders is classified as a subtype of the broader vascular collagen disease (داء الكولاجين-الوعائي) and includes specific manifestations such as lupus nephritis (التهاب الكلية الذئبي). Therapeutically, several agents are indicated for this condition: methylprednisolon acetát, methylprednisolone sodium phosphate, and hydroxychlorochin are reported to be effective treatments. Conversely, the photosensitizing agent trioxsalen and the product zlato are contraindicated in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders due to the risk of exacerbating disease activity. This integrated understanding of disease hierarchy, clinical manifestations, and appropriate versus inappropriate pharmacologic interventions guides optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1822
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders are classified as a subtype of داء الكولاجين-الوعائي (vascular collagen disease). Clinically, this group of autoimmune conditions is associated with اختلال وظيفة الكلى (kidney dysfunction), reflecting the frequent renal involvement seen in affected patients. Certain agents are contraindicated in the presence of systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders; specifically, the photosensitizing drug trioxsalen and the medication zlato should be avoided because they may exacerbate disease activity or trigger adverse reactions. Conversely, hydroxychlorochin (hydroxychloroquine) is recognized as a therapeutic option that may help control disease manifestations and improve outcomes for individuals with systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2686
retrieved_docs.content: Trioxsalen and Zlato are both contraindicated for use in patients who have systemic lupus erythematosus and other connective tissue disorders, meaning that these medications should be avoided in individuals with these autoimmune conditions due to the heightened risk of adverse reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1797
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders are classified as a subtype of داء الكولاجين-الوعائي (vascular collagen disease) and are clinically associated with اختلال وظيفة الكلى (kidney dysfunction). A specific manifestation of this renal involvement is التهاب الكلية الذئبي (lupus nephritis), which represents the renal child condition of the broader disease entity. In terms of pharmacologic management, hydroxychlorochin (hydroxychloroquine) may be used to treat systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, whereas the agents trioxsalen and zlato are contraindicated in patients with these disorders. Recognizing the hierarchical relationship, the renal complications, and the appropriate versus inappropriate medications is essential for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2826
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders are classified under the broader category of داء الكولاجين-الوعائي (vascular collagen disease). Clinically, these disorders are closely associated with اختلال وظيفة الكلى (renal dysfunction), indicating that kidney involvement is a common manifestation. Consequently, the medication zlato is contraindicated in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, reflecting the need to avoid this drug to prevent adverse effects in this patient population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2473
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders is classified as a subtype of داء الكولاجين-الوعائي (vascular collagen disease). Clinically, this autoimmune condition is frequently associated with اختلال وظيفة الكلى (kidney dysfunction), reflecting its potential impact on renal function. Therapeutically, methylprednisolon acetát and hydroxychlorochin (hydroxychloroquine) are recognized as agents that may treat systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, helping to control disease activity. Conversely, the use of trioxsalen and zlato is contraindicated in patients with this condition, as these drugs can exacerbate disease manifestations. Understanding these relationships guides clinicians in selecting appropriate treatments while avoiding contraindicated medications, thereby optimizing patient care for individuals with systemic lupus erythematosus and related connective tissue disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2076
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders is classified as a subtype of the broader category داء الكولاجين-الوعائي (collagen vascular disease). In patients with this condition, the use of trioxsalen and the medication identified as zlato is contraindicated because they may exacerbate disease activity. Conversely, therapeutic agents such as hydroxychlorochin (hydroxychloroquine) and methylprednisolone sodium succinate are considered potential treatments that can help control the manifestations of systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, contributing to improved disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2420
retrieved_docs.content: Other circulatory disease is clinically associated with systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمراء المجموعية), indicating that patients with systemic lupus erythematosus may experience or be predisposed to a range of circulatory system disorders. This relationship underscores the importance of cardiovascular assessment and monitoring in the management of systemic lupus erythematosus to identify and address potential circulatory complications promptly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: with systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمراء المجموعية). Acute and unspecified renal failure is reported to overlap with terminology for lower‑agent entities (كلاء الوكيل السفلي), indicating a need for clarification in clinical documentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 1293
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders is classified as a subtype of the broader collagen‑vascular disease category (داء الكولاجين-الوعائي). Several immunomodulatory and anti‑inflammatory agents are considered therapeutic options for this condition. Prednisone, triamcinolonacetonid (triamcinolone acetonide), and hydroxychlorochin (hydroxychloroquine) may be used to treat systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, reflecting their roles in suppressing autoimmune activity and reducing disease‑related inflammation. Conversely, trioxsalen and zlato are contraindicated in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, indicating that these agents should be avoided because of potential adverse effects or disease exacerbation. Understanding both the beneficial treatments and the contraindicated substances is essential for safe and effective management of patients with this collagen‑vascular disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders is classified as a subtype of vascular collagen disease (داء الكولاجين-الوعائي) and is clinically associated with kidney dysfunction (اختلال وظيفة الكلى). Therapeutic options that may be effective for this condition include the corticosteroid derivative triamcinolonacetonid and the antimalarial agent hydroxychlorochin (hydroxychloroquine). Conversely, the photosensitizing drug trioxsalen and the medication zlato are contraindicated in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus and connective tissue disorders, reflecting the need to avoid agents that can exacerbate disease activity or organ involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1148
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمراء المجموعية) is clinically associated with other circulatory disease, and this category of circulatory disease is classified as a subtype of connective tissue disorder (اضطراب الأنسجة الضامة). This relationship underscores that cardiovascular manifestations observed in lupus are part of the broader spectrum of connective tissue diseases, highlighting the importance of integrated cardiovascular evaluation and management in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus.
